target_game.name: Crowd City
target_game.core_loop: Run around the city gathering neutral people to grow your crowd, then consume smaller crowds to become the largest group before time runs out.
target_game.audience_proxy: Broad, casual audience of all ages looking for quick, competitive, and satisfying gameplay.
target_game.visual_style: Low-poly, colorful 3D environments with simple, stickman-like character models.

palette.description: Bright, contrasting primary colors used to distinguish different player crowds against a more muted, pastel city background.

screenshot_signals: Skin unlock screen with various characters (dinosaur, deer, penguin, etc.)

top_archetypes.label: Curiosity · animation

centroid_hook.summary: Detailed cross-section views of elaborate underground bunkers hidden beneath famous landmarks and everyday locations.
centroid_hook.visual_action: The camera reveals multi-level subterranean living spaces beneath the Eiffel Tower and a neon-lit city square.

top_archetypes.rationale: 1 creatives across 1 unique advertisers, average age 328d (velocity source: sov). Hook representative: "Detailed cross-section views of elaborate underground bunkers hidden beneath fam"

centroid_hook.text_overlay: Hey! Played a game that Actually makes you Feel relaxed?
centroid_hook.voiceover_transcript: Hey! Have you ever played a game that actually makes you feel relaxed?

centroid_hook.visual_action: A hand cursor selects a purple bus to pick up passengers, then attempts to move a blue bus that is blocked.
centroid_hook.text_overlay: WHY THIS LEVEL IS SO HARD, CAN YOU WIN?

top_archetypes.label: Satisfaction · live-action-UGC

centroid_hook.summary: A young man lying on a bed playing a game, appearing visibly happier due to his progress.
centroid_hook.visual_action: Man lying on his back on a bed, holding a tablet and playing a game with text overlays.
centroid_hook.text_overlay: "You look happier" / "Thanks I've been playing Foodie Sizzle all day and finally reached level 55!"

top_archetypes.rationale: 2 creatives across 1 unique advertisers, average age 129d (velocity source: sov). Hook representative: "A young man lying on a bed playing a game, appearing visibly happier due to his "

game_fit_scores.rationale: Major frictions: the archetype's muted dark/earth palette (navy, ochre, green) and detailed cross-section animation style clash hard with Crowd City's bright primary-color, low-poly stickman aesthetic, and "hidden bunkers" curiosity bait has no plausible bridge to a swarm/consume crowd loop. The only weak overlap is the Eiffel Tower landmark (which Crowd City does feature) and a broad casual curiosity-driven audience — but you'd need to fully reskin the hook into "what's hiding beneath the city your crowd is taking over?" to make it work, which is a heavy creative lift on a single-advertiser, low-velocity reference. Recommend skipping unless testing a bespoke landmark-reveal variant tied directly to crowd gameplay.

The "relaxing bus-clearing puzzle" hook is fundamentally misaligned with Crowd City's energetic, competitive swarm-and-chase loop — promising relaxation would mis-set expectations and tank post-install retention/IPM quality. Visuals (top-down grid puzzle, stylus interaction, muted blues) clash with Crowd City's bright primary-color crowds and 3D city chase action; only the broad casual hypercasual audience overlaps. Do not adapt this archetype as-is; if testing the "satisfaction" emotional pitch, rebuild around a swarm-growth ASMR angle (crowd snowballing, consuming rivals) rather than puzzle-clearing.

This is a live-action POV "rush home to play" hook built explicitly around Tetris Block Party — the palette (purple/gold/white), block-puzzle context, and solo cozy framing have nothing to do with Crowd City's bright primary-color, low-poly stickman swarm loop. Mechanic fit is near-zero: there's no way to demo crowd-gathering or consume-to-grow satisfaction inside this format without rebuilding the creative from scratch. Audience overlap exists at the broad-casual level, but a single-creative cluster from one advertiser with no proven crowd-genre lift makes this a low-priority test — skip unless you reshoot a Crowd City–specific POV variant.

The archetype's hook is built around a puzzle/parking-style "blocked path" fail — a static, tap-to-solve mechanic that has no analog in Crowd City's continuous swarm-and-consume loop, so adapting it would feel deceptive (classic fake-ad risk). Visually the muted bus/grid palette and hand-cursor framing clash with Crowd City's bright primary-color crowd swarms and free-roam city camera. Audience overlap exists at the broad hypercasual level, but mechanic and visual friction are severe — do not greenlight unless reworked into a "crowd gets stuck/outnumbered, can you win?" fail scenario using actual gameplay footage.

Live-action UGC testimonial bypasses Crowd City's biggest creative asset — the visceral, instantly-readable visual of a swarming stickman crowd consuming rivals — so the hook does almost no work to communicate the core loop. The muted bedroom palette and "reached level 55" progression framing also misalign with a hypercasual game that has no meaningful long-term progression and sells on 5-second satisfaction clips, not retention bragging. Audience skew (broad casual) is the only mild positive; recommend skipping unless reshot with on-screen gameplay B-roll showing the crowd-swarm payoff.

brief.title: Snowball ASMR: One Red Dot Eats the City
brief.hook_3s: A single red stickman jogs down a pastel street — tap, and 200 bystanders flip red in a satisfying wave.
scene_flow: 0–3s: Top-down close-up of ONE lone red stickman on a muted pastel plaza; finger taps screen, nearby neutrals instantly convert red with a soft 'pop' SFX cascade.
3–7s: Camera pulls back as the red crowd snowballs through low-poly streets, sweeping past parked cars and benches — counter ticks 12 → 84 → 210 in sync with ASMR footstep rumble.
7–11s: Red wave slams into a smaller BLUE crowd; blues flee, get swallowed, flip red one-by-one. Counter explodes past 400. Timer in HUD shows 0:30.
11–14s: Massive red ocean of stickmen floods past the Eiffel Tower landmark, consuming a green rival crowd in one satisfying gulp. Score: #1.
14–15s: Freeze on victory crowd; CTA button pulses.
brief.visual_direction: Authentic Crowd City top-down/3-quarter camera with the signature low-poly pastel city (cream sidewalks, soft beige buildings, mint trees) so the bright primary-red crowd reads as the only saturated element on screen. Lean into the existing HUD chrome: top-left timer, top-right player-count score bubbles in red/blue/green, bottom progress bar. Motion language is swarm-flow — wave-like conversions, fleeing blue stragglers, green crowds getting engulfed — shot to feel ASMR-satisfying (smooth camera glides, no jump cuts), contrasting the competitive chase mechanic with a calm, hypnotic pacing. Palette discipline: #FF0000 hero crowd dominates, #0000FF and #00FF00 rivals as shrinking accents, pastel city as neutral canvas.
text_overlays: Can 1 person take the whole city?
Eat the blues. Eat the greens.
brief.rationale: Voodoo's current Crowd City rotation is all no-copy video + one playable with zero narrative hook — they're not exploiting the trending 'satisfaction/ASMR' emotional pitch at all. This brief brings that winning archetype but rebuilt around swarm-snowballing (not puzzle-clearing), so it fits Crowd City's actual core loop and won't mis-set expectations like a literal relaxation promise would. UA team: test this against the silent videos as the copy-led, emotion-led variant — expect lift on CTR from the '1 person → whole city' curiosity gap and stronger IPM from accurate gameplay representation.

brief.title: Cornered Crowd — Can You Escape?
brief.hook_3s: A massive red crowd sprints around a Paris corner — and slams face-to-face with a BIGGER blue crowd. Score freezes. "WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD?"
scene_flow: 0-3s: Top-down chase cam — player's RED crowd of ~80 stickmen swarms down a pastel low-poly Paris street, gobbling neutral grey civilians, counter ticking up fast.
3-6s: Camera whips around the Eiffel Tower corner — a BLUE crowd of 180 fills the screen. Red crowd skids, score counter flashes, overlay: 'WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD?'
6-10s: Fake-fail beat — blue crowd chases red, red shrinks from 80→22, timer in red. Overlay: 'CAN YOU WIN?'
10-13s: Player pivots, ducks into an alley, scoops a hidden GREEN neutral cluster, snowballs back to 200+. Crowd recolors red as it consumes blue stragglers.
13-15s: Triumphant zoom out: red crowd floods the plaza under the Eiffel Tower, 'LARGEST CROWD' banner, CTA card.
brief.visual_direction: Authentic Crowd City top-down/3-quarter chase camera over a low-poly pastel Paris (cream buildings, mint trees, soft grey pavement). Player crowd in saturated #FF0000 red stickmen, rival crowd in pure #0000FF blue, neutral pickups glowing #00FF00 green. Keep the native HUD: top-center timer, crowd-size counter above each swarm, level pill, pause icon top-left. Motion is continuous swarm flow — no hand cursor, no tap UI, no static puzzle framing. Punchy zoom-ins on the score collision and the comeback moment to sell the fail-then-win arc.
text_overlays: WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD?
brief.rationale: Voodoo's current Crowd City rotation is all silent gameplay videos and one playable with no copy hook — there's zero "fail/challenge" framing in market. This brief imports the trending Fail · in-game archetype but reworks it from puzzle-bus-blocked into an honest Crowd City scenario (outnumbered swarm, comeback alley pickup), avoiding fake-ad risk while adding the curiosity-driven hook copy the rotation is missing. UA: A/B test against the Applovin silent video as control; expect lift on IPM from the question-overlay stop-scroll.
scenario_prompts: Hero frame, aspect 9:16, top-down 3-quarter mobile game screenshot of Crowd City gameplay: a massive swarm of low-poly stickman figures in bright #FF0000 red running through a pastel low-poly Paris street, colliding head-on with a larger swarm of #0000FF blue stickman figures near the Eiffel Tower, scattered #00FF00 green neutral stickmen on sidewalks, low-poly colorful 3D environment with simple stickman-like character models, native game HUD with top-center countdown timer and white crowd-size counters '82' and '184' floating above each crowd, level pill 'LVL 7' top-left, bold white sans-serif overlay text 'WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD?' across upper third, energetic competitive mood, no hand cursor, no tap indicators

brief.title: "You Look Happier" — Crowd City UGC Testimonial
brief.hook_3s: Friend on couch: "You look happier lately…" — cut to phone screen exploding with a red stickman swarm devouring a blue crowd.
scene_flow: 0-3s: Selfie-style vertical shot, young woman lounging on bed grinning at her phone. Friend off-camera: 'Okay… why do you look so happy today?'
3-7s: She flips the phone to camera — full-screen Crowd City gameplay: a red stickman swarm sprinting through a pastel low-poly city, snowballing neutral pedestrians (counter ticks 47 → 112 → 240).
7-11s: Quick gameplay montage — red crowd chases and consumes a smaller blue crowd, screen flashes green '+58'. She giggles: 'I made the BIGGEST crowd in the city.'
11-14s: Final beat — timer hits 0:00, red crowd dominates the map, 'WINNER' banner pops. She shrugs at camera: 'It's stupid. I can't stop.'
14-15s: End card — game logo, finger-tap CTA, app store badge.
brief.visual_direction: Phone-shot UGC aesthetic (slight handheld jitter, natural bedroom lighting, no studio polish) for the live-action wrapper, cutting hard to vibrant in-engine Crowd City footage. The swap from desaturated bedroom to the saturated #FF0000 red swarm vs #0000FF blue rivals against pastel low-poly buildings is the entire visual punchline — the contrast sells the dopamine hit. Keep #00FF00 reserved for score pop-ups and the WINNER flash. Low-poly stickman crowd must read instantly: dense, chunky, swarming.
text_overlays: why do you look so happy?
brief.rationale: Voodoo's current Crowd City rotation is 100% pure-gameplay video and playables with zero copy or human presence — there is no UGC/testimonial angle in market. This brief imports the trending live-action "you look happier" satisfaction hook but fixes the archetype's biggest weakness (no gameplay payoff) by front-loading the phone-flip reveal so the swarm-consume loop lands inside 5 seconds. UA team: test against the Applovin video baseline on TikTok/Reels placements where UGC outperforms polished gameplay creatives.
